topic: Two colleagues are both considered for the same promotion
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stared at the framed memo on his desk, the glossy paper reflecting the harsh fluorescent light above. Sarah’s name sat prominently at the top, the coveted Senior Analyst position. He picked up his lukewarm coffee mug, the ceramic suddenly feeling slick and cold in his grip. Every time he glanced towards the office door, he could almost hear the faint, rhythmic tapping of Sarah’s keyboard—a sound that seemed to echo the silence where his own ambition used to reside. He spent the rest of the afternoon meticulously organizing files, a task so mundane it felt like a desperate attempt to impose order on a chaotic internal landscape. When his supervisor finally called him in, Elias managed a smile that felt stretched too thin across his face. "Congratulations, Elias. I trust you'll be taking on new responsibilities." The words tasted like ash. He nodded, the movement stiff, and focused intently on the texture of the carpet beneath his worn shoes, trying to anchor himself against the sudden, dizzying shift in the room's gravity.

[story 2]

"It's a tough competition, isn't it?" Clara murmured, swirling the ice in her water glass, her eyes fixed on the distant cityscape visible through the window. Liam felt a knot tightening in his chest, a cold, heavy pressure that made his breath catch in his throat. He kept replaying the feedback session from Tuesday—Sarah’s articulate presentation versus his own, which he felt had been competent but ultimately lacking that spark. He started pacing the length of his cubicle, his movements jerky and too fast. Each footfall seemed to generate a loud, unwelcome noise in the quiet office. When his manager approached, Clara immediately shifted her posture, leaning forward with an exaggerated air of eagerness. Liam just managed a weak nod, his gaze darting everywhere but directly at his superior, focusing instead on the slight tremor in his own hands as he gripped the edge of his desk.

[story 3]

I found myself tracing the outline of the promotion announcement on my screen, my finger hovering just above the bolded title. The air in the shared workspace felt thick, charged with unspoken comparison. Every time I looked over at Mark, he was laughing easily with a colleague, his posture radiating an effortless confidence I simply didn't possess right now. My jaw felt tight, a constant clenching that made my molars ache. I started fiddling with the alignment of my monitor, subtly adjusting the angle until the reflection of my own face seemed distorted and unfamiliar. When the meeting finally arrived, I spoke clearly, perhaps too loudly, about my qualifications. The room seemed to shrink around me, and I could feel a distinct sense of isolation, as if I were watching a play where I was the only actor who hadn't received the leading role.
